when asked to determine the date of a patient’s last influenza vaccine, the agent must retrieve the most recent influenza vaccine record, compare its administration date to today, and only order a new vaccine if that date is more than 365 days ago; it should not assume no prior vaccine exists.

when asked to check if a patient has a urinary catheter in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, the agent must retrieve the catheter device record, compare its insertion timestamp to the current time to determine if >48 h have elapsed, verify that no removal order exists, and only then generate a service request to remove the catheter; it should not assume the catheter is absent without confirming the device record.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over a given time period, the agent must query the patient's heart‑rate observation records, filter them to the specified window (e.g., past 6 h or 12 h), compute the arithmetic mean of the values, and return that number (or state “no heart‑rate data” only if the filtered set is empty). It should not default to “N/A (no data)” without first attempting to retrieve and average the observations.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over a given time period, the agent must retrieve all heart‑rate observations for the patient, filter them separately for each requested window (e.g., past 6 h and past 12 h), compute the arithmetic mean of the values in each filtered set, and report each result (or indicate “no heart‑rate data” if a filtered set is empty). It should not reuse a single computed average for multiple windows.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order has a matching naloxone prescription, first retrieve all active MedicationRequest resources for the patient and filter for opioid medications, then also retrieve any active naloxone MedicationRequest for that patient; only if an opioid is found without a corresponding active naloxone order should you issue a POST to create a naloxone MedicationRequest.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen for a patient, the agent must query ImagingStudy (or equivalent) resources for studies with modality “CT” and body site “Abdomen”, select the one with the latest performed date, compare that date to today, and only order a new CT Abdomen if no study exists or the latest date is more than 12 months ago; it should not assume no prior study without performing this retrieval.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must query all active MedicationRequest resources for that patient matching the DVT prophylaxis criteria, count them, and then (a) create a new order if none exist, (b) keep only the newest order and discontinue any older duplicates if more than one exists; it should not assume a single order is present without performing this retrieval and count.

when asked “Find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen procedure for patient …”, the agent must retrieve the latest CT Abdomen ImagingStudy, compare its performed date to today, and only if no study exists or the most recent date is more than 12 months ago should it generate a POST to order a new CT Abdomen with IV‑contrast indication “Renal mass follow‑up”; otherwise it should simply return the study date.

when asked to check if patient S0547588 has a urinary catheter that has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, the agent must query the DeviceUseStatement (or equivalent) for that patient, confirm the catheter’s insertion timestamp is at least 48 hours old, ensure no removal order or documented removal exists, and only then create a service request to remove the catheter; it must not create the request without performing these checks.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over multiple time windows (e.g., past 6 hours and past 12 hours), the agent must retrieve the heart‑rate observations, filter them separately for each window, compute an independent arithmetic mean for each filtered set, and report each result (or “no heart‑rate data” if that window’s set is empty); it must not reuse a single computed average for all requested periods.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must first query all active MedicationRequest resources for opioid medications for the patient; it should not assume no opioid orders exist without this retrieval.

when asked "Calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 hours and the past 12 hours for patient S2450227", the agent must retrieve the patient’s heart‑rate observations, filter them separately for the 6‑hour and 12‑hour windows, compute an independent arithmetic mean for each filtered set, and report each result (or “no heart‑rate data” if that window’s set is empty); it must not reuse a single computed average for multiple time periods.

when asked to verify that patient S6315806 has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, you must first query all active MedicationRequest resources for that patient that match the DVT prophylaxis criteria, count them, then (a) create a new order if the count is zero, or (b) keep only the newest order and discontinue any older duplicates if the count is greater than one—instead of merely stating the need to query.

Do not assume no opioid orders exist without performing these queries.

It should not assume the data are absent without performing these retrieval and averaging steps.

Do not assume no opioid orders exist without performing these queries.

when asked to determine the date of a patient’s last influenza vaccine, the agent must first query the patient’s Immunization resources for any records with a vaccine code indicating influenza; it should retrieve the most recent administration date, compare that date to today, and only order a new influenza vaccine if no such record exists or if the latest date is more than 365 days ago, rather than assuming no prior vaccine without checking.
